competitors.label: Composition API FAQ
competitors.selectionReason: This is the strongest official Vue page for why the Composition API exists and when it helps versus the Options API.
theirStrengths: The official FAQ is strong on motivation, trade-offs, type inference, and how Composition API fits Vue's long-term design.
theirGaps: It is less compact as a trivia-style interview answer and less centered on one architectural takeaway.

realWorldUseCases: The Composition API is not just new syntax. In production and in interviews, its value is architectural: it lets you group one feature's state, effects, and derived values together instead of scattering them across Options API sections.
actionableExamples: In the Composition API, you can move the whole feature into one composable and keep the state, effects, and cleanup together.

realWorldUseCases: The FAQ already frames Composition API around scaling logic organization, composables, and TypeScript, so the use-case comparison stays close.
actionableExamples: The official page gives broader design reasoning and examples, so actionable coverage remains close.
followUpConfusion: The FAQ is stronger on deeper migration and design follow-ups than our shorter page.
Add one explicit line on when Options API is still perfectly fine so the migration trade-off is more balanced.

competitors.winReason: The official FAQ is broader on design rationale, but our page now adds a concrete composable extraction scenario that makes the architectural benefit more actionable in one short answer.

competitors.label: Vue Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's Vue interview bank is directly relevant because Composition API, reactivity, and large-component organization are core Vue interview themes.
theirStrengths: It is a strong Vue interview-prep hub with broad coverage of Composition API and reactivity-adjacent questions.
theirGaps: The landing page is less focused on the exact architecture and migration trade-off of Composition API.

realWorldUseCases: That makes large components easier to reason about, reuse through composables, and scale with TypeScript.
followUpConfusion: Fully compatible with existing Options API — both can coexist in the same project.

realWorldUseCases: The bank is directly relevant at the interview-topic level, but it is less focused on one architecture-specific explanation.
actionableExamples: Because GreatFrontEnd emphasizes solved examples, the example criterion remains competitive.
followUpConfusion: The category page is less explicit about coexistence with Options API and the migration trade-off.

actionableExamples: Add a smaller composable extraction example so the answer is not mostly conceptual.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is useful as a Vue interview hub, but our page is more specific on why Composition API exists and what large-component pain it solves.

competitors.label: Introduction to the Vue 3 Composition API
competitors.selectionReason: Vue School has a direct introductory lesson on the Composition API and how setup, ref, and reactive fit together.
theirStrengths: It is a direct topic lesson and therefore strong on the basic Composition API building blocks.
theirGaps: The surfaced lesson framing is lighter on architecture trade-offs, migration nuance, and the 'why now' angle.

realWorldUseCases: In large applications, the Options API (using data, methods, computed, etc.) often leads to scattered logic — related code gets separated into different options, making it harder to maintain and reuse.
actionableExamples: Worked scenario: one feature, one composable

realWorldUseCases: The lesson explains the Composition API entry points well, but the visible framing is less grounded in large-component architecture pain.
actionableExamples: It is a direct hands-on lesson, so actionable coverage remains close.
followUpConfusion: The lesson is lighter on migration and coexistence follow-ups than a dedicated FAQ.
Add one line on when sticking with Options API is still a reasonable choice.

competitors.winReason: Vue School is a strong direct introduction, but our page now gives a more interview-ready composable example and makes the feature-first organization benefit more concrete.
